docs.title: Rivet Language Docs
docs.text: Rivet is a small, LLVM-backed systems language prototype focused on readable syntax and explicit control. These docs describe the current implementation in this repository so you can quickly understand what works today.

docs.title: What Rivet Currently Supports
docs.text:
• Integer literals and integer variables (int)
  • Variable declarations with optional initialization
  • Expression statements (for example, a + b;)
  • Arithmetic operators: +, -, *, /
  • Comparisons: ==, !=, <, >
  • Bitwise/logical keyword operators: and, or, lsft, rsft
  • Assignment with = to declared variables
  • if / else blocks
  • while loops
  • Function-call expressions (name(arg1, arg2))
  • Module imports with import module_name;
  • LLVM IR generation to a synthetic entry function __rivet_entry

docs.title: What Is Partially Implemented or Planned
docs.text:
• fun and return tokens exist in the lexer, but function declaration parsing is not part of the current parser flow.
  • Unary expression codegen is not implemented.
  • Arrays are not implemented yet.
  • Import resolution currently tries:
    • lib/<module>.rvt
    • ../lib/<module>.rvt (fallback for build-directory execution)

docs.title: Typical Compile Flow
docs.text:
1. Lexing: source file is tokenized with line/column tracking.
  2. Parsing: recursive-descent parser builds AST nodes.
  3. AST dump (optional): run with --dump-ast to inspect parse output.
  4. Codegen: AST emits LLVM IR into module Rivet Bare Metal Module.
  5. IR print: module is printed to stdout.

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
import dummy;

int i = 0;
while (i < 3) {
        i = i + 1;
}

i + imported_x;

Run from build/:

./rivet ../tests/import_dummy_test.rvt --dump-ast

docs.title: Reading Guide
docs.text:
• Start with Language Basics for day-to-day syntax.
  • Use Reference for compact grammar and keyword tables.
  • Use Internals to understand parser and LLVM backend behavior.

docs.title: Control Flow
docs.text: Rivet currently supports block-based if/else and while.

docs.title: if / else
docs.text:
Syntax:

if (<expression>) {
    <statements>
} else {
    <statements>
}

docs.title: Current parser behavior
docs.text:
• if condition must be wrapped in parentheses.
  • then and else are parsed as blocks ({ ... }) in the current implementation.
  • else is optional.

docs.title: Truthiness in codegen
docs.text:
Conditions are lowered to integer comparison against zero:

  • non-zero -> true
  • zero -> false

docs.title: while
docs.text:
Syntax:

while (<expression>) {
    <statements>
}

docs.title: Current parser behavior
docs.text:
• Condition must be in (...).
  • Body is parsed as a block.

docs.title: LLVM structure generated
docs.text:
Codegen creates three blocks:

  1. condition block
  2. loop body block
  3. after-loop block

The body branches back to the condition block.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
int i = 0;

while (i < 10) {
    if (i == 5) {
        i = i + 2;
    } else {
        i = i + 1;
    }
}

i;

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• No break / continue yet.
  • No single-line non-block if body form in parser path right now.
  • No for loop support yet.

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: This page separates what is supported today from what is planned.

docs.title: Function call expressions
docs.text:
Parser supports calls in expression position:

do_work();
mix(a, b, 3);

A call is parsed when an identifier is followed by (. Arguments are parsed as comma-separated expressions.

docs.title: Call codegen behavior
docs.text:
During codegen, Rivet:

  1. Looks up the callee by name in the LLVM module.
  2. Verifies argument count.
  3. Emits call instruction.

If function is missing or arity mismatches, codegen reports an error.

docs.title: Function declarations
docs.text: Tokens for fun and return exist, and docs grammar mentions function declarations. However, parser flow in the current implementation is focused on statements/imports/variables/control-flow. Treat function declaration syntax as in-progress unless you add parser + AST + codegen paths for it.

docs.title: Practical Guidance
docs.text:
• Use calls only when callee functions are present in the LLVM module.
  • For quick experiments, most current tests focus on top-level statements and generated __rivet_entry.

docs.title: Example Call Form
docs.text:
import math;

int x = 7;
int y = 3;
add(x, y);

docs.title: Variables
docs.text: This page describes variable behavior in the current Rivet implementation.

docs.title: Declaration Forms
docs.text:
Rivet currently supports int variables with two forms:

int x;
int y = 42;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• int x; is valid and defaults to 0 during codegen.
  • int y = <expression>; evaluates the expression and stores the result.
  • Declarations must end with ;.

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text:
Assignment works as a binary expression with =:

int i = 0;
i = i + 1;

Current rule: the left-hand side must be a declared variable. If the left side is not a variable node, codegen reports an error.

docs.title: Reading Variables
docs.text:
Using a variable in an expression generates a load from its stack slot:

int a = 7;
int b = 3;
a + b;

docs.title: Scope Model (Current)
docs.text:
Variables are stored in a shared name map for codegen. Practical implication in the current prototype:

  • Redeclaration and shadowing rules are not fully formalized yet.
  • Keep names unique in small programs to avoid confusion.

docs.title: Supported Expression Operators with Variables
docs.text:
• Arithmetic: +, -, *, /
  • Equality: ==, !=
  • Relational: <, >
  • Keyword logical/bitwise: and, or, lsft, rsft

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
int x = 10;
int y = 2;

x = x + y;
x = x lsft 1;
x;

docs.title: LLVM Backend Details
docs.text: This page explains how Rivet lowers language constructs into LLVM IR today.

docs.title: Module and Entry Function
docs.text:
Codegen initializes:

  • LLVMContext
  • IRBuilder<>
  • Module named Rivet Bare Metal Module

Top-level code is emitted into:

define i32 @__rivet_entry() {
entry:
    ...
}

docs.title: Variable Lowering
docs.text:
Declaration:

int x = 7;

Becomes roughly:

%x = alloca i32
store i32 7, ptr %x

Variable read uses load.

docs.title: Binary Operators
docs.text:
Current mapping:

  • + -> CreateAdd
  • - -> CreateSub
  • * -> CreateMul
  • / -> CreateSDiv
  • and -> CreateAnd
  • or -> CreateOr
  • lsft -> CreateShl
  • rsft -> CreateAShr
  • == -> icmp eq then zero-extend to i32
  • != -> icmp ne then zero-extend to i32
  • < -> icmp slt then zero-extend to i32
  • > -> icmp sgt then zero-extend to i32
  • = -> store into variable slot and return RHS value

docs.title: If/Else
docs.text:
If creates blocks:

  • then
  • else
  • ifcont

Condition is tested against zero for branch selection.

docs.title: While
docs.text:
While creates blocks:

  • cond
  • loop
  • afterloop

Flow:

  1. branch to cond
  2. evaluate condition
  3. branch to loop or afterloop
  4. loop body branches back to cond

docs.title: Imports and IR
docs.text: ImportAST::codegen() codegens all imported AST nodes in sequence before continuing with the importing file. This effectively injects imported declarations/statements into the same module.

docs.title: Current Backend Limitations
docs.text:
• Unary operations are not lowered yet.
  • More advanced type system and arrays are not implemented.
  • Function declaration pipeline is incomplete in parser/codegen integration.

docs.title: Internals Overview
docs.text: This page documents the actual compiler pipeline as implemented in this repository.

docs.title: High-Level Architecture
docs.text:
Rivet currently uses a classic front-end + LLVM IR backend design:

  1. Lexer (src/Lexer.cpp)
  2. Parser (src/Parser.cpp)
  3. AST (include/Rivet/AST.h, src/AST.cpp)
  4. Codegen Context (include/Rivet/CodeGen.h, src/CodeGen.cpp)
  5. Driver (src/main.cpp)

docs.title: Lexer
docs.text:
The lexer reads a file stream and returns integer tokens.

  • Keywords are negative enum values (for example tok_if, tok_import).
  • Punctuation/operators such as +, =, <, ( are returned as ASCII values.
  • Supports line comments // ....
  • Tracks CurrentLine and CurrentColumn for diagnostics.

docs.title: Parser
docs.text: Parser is recursive descent with operator-precedence parsing for binary expressions.

docs.title: Statement entry points
docs.text:
Current statement parsing includes:

  • import <id>;
  • variable declarations (int ...)
  • if (...) { ... } [else { ... }]
  • while (...) { ... }
  • blocks { ... }
  • expression statements
  • empty statement ;

docs.title: Expression parsing
docs.text:
• primary: number, identifier/call, parenthesized expression
  • binary RHS parser (ParseBinOpRHS) with precedence table

docs.title: Import flow
docs.text:
ParseImport parses module name, avoids duplicate imports, and recursively parses imported source file into an ImportAST node.

Path strategy currently:

  1. lib/<module>.rvt
  2. fallback ../lib/<module>.rvt

docs.title: AST Layer
docs.text:
Main nodes currently present:

  • NumberAST
  • VariableAST
  • VariableDeclAST
  • BinaryOpAST
  • BlockAST
  • IfAST
  • WhileAST
  • CallAST
  • ImportAST

Each node provides:

  • dump() for debug tree output
  • codegen() for LLVM generation

docs.title: Driver (main)
docs.text:
The driver:

  1. Loads source file.
  2. Parses to top-level AST node list.
  3. Optionally dumps AST.
  4. Creates LLVM module context.
  5. Builds synthetic entry function __rivet_entry.
  6. Codegens each top-level node.
  7. Emits ret if current block has no terminator.
  8. Prints full LLVM IR.

docs.title: Keywords and Tokens
docs.text: Rivet uses negative integer token values for language keywords and dynamic token categories. Single-character punctuation/operators are returned as ASCII integer values.

docs.title: Dynamic Tokens
docs.text: Category Token Value Meaning identifier -2 Variable/function/module names number -3 Integer literal

docs.title: Types and Memory Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Token Value Status int -10 Implemented void -11 Tokenized; parser integration limited ref -12 Tokenized; parser integration limited address_of -13 Tokenized; parser integration limited deref -14 Tokenized; parser integration limited

docs.title: Logical and Bitwise Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Token Value Notes and -20 Parsed as binary operator or -21 Parsed as binary operator not -22 Token exists; unary path not fully wired in codegen lsft -23 Parsed as shift-left-like operator rsft -24 Parsed as arithmetic shift-right-like operator == -25 Equality compare != -26 Not-equal compare

docs.title: Control/Module Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Token Value Status if -30 Implemented else -31 Implemented while -32 Implemented fun -33 Tokenized; full function-declaration flow is in progress return -34 Tokenized; return statement flow is in progress import -35 Implemented

docs.title: ASCII Operator Tokens
docs.text:
These are returned directly as character code values by the lexer:

  • = (61)
  • < (60)
  • > (62)
  • + (43)
  • - (45)
  • * (42)
  • / (47)
  • (, ), {, }, ,, ;

This is why AST debug output may show numbers like 43 for + and 61 for =.

docs.title: Syntax Reference
docs.text: This page is a practical syntax reference for the current Rivet implementation.

docs.title: Program Shape
docs.text:
A file can contain top-level items such as:

  • import statements
  • variable declarations
  • control-flow statements
  • expression statements

docs.title: Import
docs.text: import dummy;

docs.title: Variable declaration
docs.text:
int x;
int y = 10;

docs.title: If / Else
docs.text:
if (x == 0) {
    x = x + 1;
} else {
    x = x + 2;
}

docs.title: While
docs.text:
while (x < 10) {
    x = x + 1;
}

docs.title: Expression statement
docs.text:
x + y;
call_me(x);

docs.title: Empty statement
docs.text: ;

docs.title: Expressions
docs.text:
Primary forms:

  • number literal
  • identifier
  • function call
  • parenthesized expression

Supported binary operators:

  • assignment: =
  • relational: <, >
  • equality: ==, !=
  • arithmetic: +, -, *, /
  • keyword operators: and, or, lsft, rsft

docs.title: Calls
docs.text:
sum(a, b, 3);

Arguments are comma-separated expressions.

docs.title: Notes on Current Parsing Rules
docs.text:
• Conditions for if/while require parentheses.
  • Bodies are currently handled as block forms.
  • Every declaration/statement above ends with ; except block/control structures.
